Long, long ago, before the sky had color, the world was a canvas of endless, suffocating gray. High above the highest peaks lived Aethelgard, the cosmic weaver. He was a solitary god with fingers spun from pure starlight. For eons, Aethelgard watched the mortals below stumble in the gloom, their hearts heavy with the monotony of their twilight existence.

Pity blossomed in his incredibly ancient chest. He decided to craft a masterpiece to bring them hope. Aethelgard gathered the most precious materials of the cosmos: the furious red of dying suns, the deep emerald of newborn nebulas, and the shimmering violet of comet dust. He strung his colossal loom between the two poles of the earth.

For seven nights and seven days, he wove. His hands moved with blinding speed, throwing the celestial shuttle back and forth across the heavens. The threads of light intertwined, dancing and rippling in the dark.

When he finally finished, he breathed life into the tapestry. The sky erupted in a cascading ribbon of brilliant, shifting colors. Today, we call it the Aurora Borealis, but the ancients knew it was simply Aethelgard, forever weaving his luminous promise that light will always conquer the absolute dark.

A sudden, bright spark of magic, born of her profound sorrow for the lost treasure, shattered the water's curse.
